title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: psvr R package implementing four SVR models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. targets forecasting contexts targets yk > 0 strictly positive relative accuracy matters absolute accuracy. Source paper: Unified Family Percentage-Error Support Vector Regression Models Symmetric Kernel Extensions (MDPI Mathematics, 2026). Full derivations paper.tex repository root.

what: Model 1 — ε-SVR with MAPE (Theorem 1 / Appendix A.1)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Primal: Dual QP (max form): βk = αk - αk*, |βk| = αk + αk*. KKT: ω = Σk βk φ(xk), Σk βk = 0, 0 ≤ αk, αk* ≤ 100C/yk. Prediction: f(x) = Σk βk K(xk, x) + b Implementation: QP via osqp. Key files: R/mape_svr.R.
code: min  ½‖ω‖² + C Σk (ξk + ξk*) s.t. (100/yk)(yk - ωᵀφ(xk) - b) ≤ ε + ξk      (100/yk)(ωᵀφ(xk) + b - yk) ≤ ε + ξk*      ξk, ξk* ≥ 0 max  -½ Σk,l βk βl K(xk,xl) + Σk βk yk - (ε/100) Σk |βk| yk s.t. Σk βk = 0      |βk| ≤ 100C / yk    (sample-dependent box; yk small → tight bound)

what: Model 2 — Symmetric ε-SVR with MAPE (Theorem 2 / Appendix A.2)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: primal Model 1, augmented symmetry constraint: ωᵀφ(xk) = · ωᵀφ(-xk) Dual QP (max form): Ks(xi,xj) = K(xi,xj) + ·K(xi,-xj). factor ¼ (vs ½ Model 1) arises symmetrization representer theorem (Appendix .2): ωᵀφ(xk) = ½ Σl βl Ks(xl, xk), quadratic term picks extra ½. Prediction: f(x) = ½ Σk βk Ks(xk, x) + b Implementation: QP via osqp. Build Ks = Ω + ·Ω* inline (½), pass P = ¼·Ks QP quadratic matrix — ¼ factor absorbed P directly. use sym_kernel_matrix() ; helper returns ½(Ω + ·Ω*) = Ωs, correct Model 4. Key files: R/mape_sym_svr.R.
code: max  -¼ Σk,l βk βl Ks(xk,xl) + Σk βk yk - (ε/100) Σk |βk| yk s.t. Σk βk = 0      |βk| ≤ 100C / yk    (unchanged from Model 1)

what: Model 3 — LS-SVR with RMSPE (Theorem 3 / Appendix A.3)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Primal: KKT stationarity: - ω = Σk αk φ(xk) - Σk αk = 0 - ek = (yk²/Γ) αk Linear system (solve directly): YΓ = diag(y1²/Γ, …, yN²/Γ) — added diagonal Ω . Prediction: f(x) = Σk αk K(xk, x) + b Implementation: base::solve() (N+1)×(N+1) augmented matrix. Key files: R/rmspe_lssvr.R.
code: min  ½‖ω‖² + (Γ/2) Σk ek²/yk² s.t. yk = ωᵀφ(xk) + b + ek [ 0    1ᵀ      ] [b]   [0] [ 1    Ω + YΓ  ] [α] = [y]

what: Model 4 — Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE (Theorem 4 / Appendix A.4)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: primal Model 3, augmented symmetry constraint. Linear system: Ωs = ½(Ω + aΩ*), Ω*kl = K(xk, -xl). Symmetric representer: ωᵀφ(xk) = Σl (Ωs)kl αl Prediction: f(x) = ½ Σk αk Ks(xk, x) + b equivalently: f(x) = Σk αk [K(xk,x) + ·K(xk,-x)] / 2 + b Implementation: base::solve() Ωs replacing Ω. Key files: R/rmspe_sym_lssvr.R.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Required Models 2 4: - K(-xi, xj) = K(xi, -xj) - K(-xi, -xj) = K(xi, xj) RBF kernel K(xi, xj) = exp(-‖xi - xj‖²/2σ²) satisfies conditions: - K(-xi, -xj) = exp(-‖-xi-(-xj)‖²/2σ²) = exp(-‖xi-xj‖²/2σ²) = K(xi,xj) ✓ - K(-xi, xj) = exp(-‖-xi-xj‖²/2σ²) = exp(-‖xi+xj‖²/2σ²) = K(xi,-xj) ✓ Polynomial kernels even degree also satisfy Assumption 3.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: QP solver: osqp package (Models 1 & 2). Supports sparse matrices, commercial license. Linear solver: base::solve() (Models 3 & 4). Dense (N+1)×(N+1) system, extra dep. Target validation: fit() functions call stopifnot((y > 0)). Kernel interface: make_kernel(type, ...) returns closure function(xi, xj) type ∈ {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. Shared across four models. YΓ storage: stored vector y^2 / gamma; added diagonal via diag(Omega) <- diag(Omega) + y_gamma — never materializes full N×N diagonal matrix. "psvr_mape" (Model 1) "psvr_mape_sym" (Model 2) "psvr_rmspe" (Model 3) "psvr_rmspe_sym" (Model 4) S3 methods: predict.psvr_*() class. Documentation: roxygen2 @param, @return, @examples. Style: tidyverse / base R style: snake_case, <- assignment. License: MIT.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: yk > 0 always — validated fit time, never silently coerced. ∈ {-1, 1} symmetric models. symmetric kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj) uses negation xj, kernel closure must accept negative inputs even training data positive. YΓ diagonal — add diag(Omega) place, never build N×N diagonal matrix. Box constraints Models 1 & 2 per-sample: |βk| ≤ 100C/yk. Prediction Model 2: multiply ½ (symmetric representer theorem). Prediction Model 4: use ½(K(xk,x) + ·K(xk,-x)) per support vector.

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: Electricity grid operators must predict demand days ahead schedule generation assets, balance grid, avoid costly emergency purchases. Victoria, Australia operates competitive spot market forecast errors translate directly monetary penalties — making relative accuracy natural objective. vic_elec dataset tsibbledata package records half-hourly demand temperature readings state Victoria 2012–2014. aggregate daily level: total daily demand (MWh) mean daily temperature (°C). Daily forecasting common planning horizon unit commitment fuel scheduling. Figure 1: Daily electricity demand Victoria, 2012–2014. Colour encodes mean daily temperature. summer (Dec–Feb) heat-wave spikes lower weekend/holiday baseline visible. MAPE matters Absolute-error objectives (MAE, MSE) treat days equally. grid operator’s cost function relative: 10,000 MWh error mild autumn day (baseline ≈ 165,000 MWh) 6.1% — critical: error large relative scheduled capacity. 10,000 MWh error hot summer day (baseline ≈ 305,000 MWh) 3.3% — tolerable: dwarfed total load. Minimising MAPE directly aligns model operator’s penalty structure. Figure 2: fixed 10,000 MWh absolute error different relative impact depending day’s baseline demand.
code:
elec_daily <- vic_elec |>   as_tibble() |>   mutate(Date = as.Date(Time)) |>   group_by(Date) |>   summarise(     Demand      = sum(Demand,       na.rm = TRUE),   # sum 48 half-hourly readings → MWh/day     Temperature = mean(Temperature, na.rm = TRUE),   # daily average temperature     IsHoliday   = any(Holiday),                      # TRUE if any half-hour is a public holiday     .groups = "drop"   ) |>   mutate(     Temperature2 = Temperature^2,                    # U-shaped demand-temperature relationship     DayOfWeek    = wday(Date, week_start = 1),       # Monday = 1, Sunday = 7     Month        = month(Date),     IsWeekend    = as.integer(DayOfWeek >= 6),     IsHoliday    = as.integer(IsHoliday)   )  cat(sprintf("Rows: %d  |  Dates: %s – %s
",             nrow(elec_daily), min(elec_daily$Date), max(elec_daily$Date))) Rows: 1097  |  Dates: 2011-12-31 – 2014-12-31 cat(sprintf("Demand range: %.0f – %.0f MWh/day
",             min(elec_daily$Demand), max(elec_daily$Demand))) Demand range: 82532 – 351460 MWh/day cat(sprintf("Public holidays: %d  |  Weekend days: %d
",             sum(elec_daily$IsHoliday), sum(elec_daily$IsWeekend))) Public holidays: 58  |  Weekend days: 314 elec_daily |>   ggplot(aes(x = Date, y = Demand / 1e3, colour = Temperature)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.4, alpha = 0.85) +   scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "C", name = "Temp (°C)") +   scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +   labs(x = NULL, y = "Demand (GWh/day)",        title = "Victoria daily electricity demand — 2012–2014",        subtitle = "Colour = mean daily temperature") +   theme(legend.position = "right") tibble(   baseline  = seq(80e3, 360e3, by = 1000),   abs_error = 10000,   pct_error = abs_error / baseline * 100 ) |>   ggplot(aes(baseline / 1e3, pct_error)) +   geom_line(colour = vpal[2], linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_point(     data = tibble(       baseline  = c(165e3, 305e3),       pct_error = 10000 / c(165e3, 305e3) * 100     ),     aes(baseline / 1e3, pct_error), size = 3, colour = vpal[5]   ) +   annotate("text", x = 165, y = 10000 / 165e3 * 100 + 0.5,            label = "Mild day: 6.1%", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +   annotate("text", x = 305, y = 10000 / 305e3 * 100 + 0.5,            label = "Hot day: 3.3%",  hjust = 1, size = 3.5) +   labs(x = "Baseline demand (GWh/day)", y = "Percentage error (%)",        title = "Asymmetric impact of a fixed 10 GWh error",        subtitle = "Same absolute error — very different relative cost") +   scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +   scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma())

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: Classical regression minimises residuals globally. SVR takes different approach: finds flattest function (smallest ‖ω‖²) fits training points within ε-tube. Points outside tube penalised, points — support vectors — determine solution. Figure 3: SVR 1-D synthetic dataset. Grey band: ε-insensitivity tube. Blue points: support vectors (outside boundary). fitted line determined support vectors. Mathematical detail: SVR primal formulation standard ε-SVR solves primal problem: minω,b,ξ,ξ*12∥ω∥2+C∑k=1N(ξk+ξk*) \min_{\omega, b, \xi, \xi^*} \quad \tfrac{1}{2}\|\omega\|^2   + C \sum_{k=1}^N (\xi_k + \xi_k^*) subject yk−ω⊤ϕ(xk)−b≤ε+ξkω⊤ϕ(xk)+b−yk≤ε+ξk*ξk,ξk*≥0 \begin{aligned} y_k - \omega^\top \phi(x_k) - b &\leq \varepsilon + \xi_k \\ \omega^\top \phi(x_k) + b - y_k &\leq \varepsilon + \xi_k^* \\ \xi_k,\, \xi_k^* &\geq 0 \end{aligned} ε-tube means residuals smaller ε incur zero loss — outliers contribute objective. dual reveals solution f(x)=∑kβkK(xk,x)+bf(x) = \sum_k \beta_k K(x_k, x) + b βk=αk−αk*\beta_k = \alpha_k - \alpha_k^*.
code: set.seed(7) n_syn  <- 40 x_syn  <- sort(runif(n_syn, 0, 10)) y_syn  <- 2 * x_syn + 1 + rnorm(n_syn, sd = 1.5)  df_syn   <- data.frame(x = x_syn, y = y_syn) svm_1d   <- ksvm(y ~ x, data = df_syn, type = "eps-svr",                  kernel = "vanilladot", C = 5, epsilon = 1.5, scaled = FALSE) Setting default kernel parameters pred_syn <- predict(svm_1d, df_syn) sv_flags <- seq_len(n_syn) %in% SVindex(svm_1d)  tibble(x = x_syn, y = y_syn, yhat = as.numeric(pred_syn), is_sv = sv_flags) |>   ggplot(aes(x, y)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = yhat - 1.5, ymax = yhat + 1.5),               fill = "grey85", alpha = 0.6) +   geom_line(aes(y = yhat), colour = vpal[2], linewidth = 1) +   geom_point(aes(colour = is_sv, size = is_sv), alpha = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey60", "TRUE" = vpal[5]),                       labels = c("Interior point", "Support vector"),                       name = NULL) +   scale_size_manual(values = c("FALSE" = 2, "TRUE" = 3.5), guide = "none") +   labs(x = "x", y = "y",        title = "SVR: fitting within the ε-tube",        subtitle = "Only support vectors (blue) influence the decision boundary") +   theme(legend.position = "top")

what: 3 Why percentage error changes the math
title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: classical ε-SVR, QP box constraints uniform: |βk|≤Cfor k.|\beta_k| \leq C \quad \text{} k. MAPE ε-SVR (Model 1), constraints become per-sample: |βk|≤100Cyk.|\beta_k| \leq \frac{100\,C}{y_k}. Low-demand days get tighter bounds — model devotes capacity accurately representing , exactly percentage-error objective requires. Note psvr models fitted scaled targets (see Section 4); figure uses scaled range. Figure 4: Per-sample box constraint MAPE-SVR (100C/yₖ, C = 5) versus flat classical bound. lower scaled demand (~1.6) bound tightens substantially. Mathematical detail: MAPE ε-SVR dual (Theorem 1) Replacing absolute-error tube percentage tube — (100/yk)|yk−f(xk)|≤ε(100/y_k)|y_k - f(x_k)| \leq \varepsilon — transforms dual : maxβ−12∑k,lβkβlK(xk,xl)+∑kβkyk−ε100∑k|βk|yk \max_\beta \quad -\tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{k,l} \beta_k \beta_l K(x_k, x_l)   + \sum_k \beta_k y_k   - \frac{\varepsilon}{100} \sum_k |\beta_k|\, y_k subject ∑kβk=0\sum_k \beta_k = 0 |βk|≤100C/yk|\beta_k| \leq 100C/y_k. per-sample upper bound 100C/yk100C/y_k tightens small yky_k (low-demand days), preventing model -fitting absolute fluctuations days. See Theorem 1 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) full derivation. LS-SVR variant (Model 3) replaces ε-insensitive loss squared loss, turning QP linear system. percentage-error variant modifies regularization diagonal: YΓ=diag(y12Γ,…,yN2Γ)Y_\Gamma = \text{diag}\!\left(\frac{y_1^2}{\Gamma}, \ldots, \frac{y_N^2}{\Gamma}\right) target-weighted diagonal causes system minimise root mean squared percentage error (RMSPE) rather standard squared residual. key advantage: solution requires base::solve() (N+1)×(N+1)(N+1)\times(N+1) system — QP solver needed.
code: C_val  <- 5 y_grid <- seq(1.5, 3.6, by = 0.01)   # scaled daily demand range  tibble(   y         = y_grid,   classical = C_val,   mape_svr  = 100 * C_val / y_grid ) |>   tidyr::pivot_longer(-y, names_to = "model", values_to = "bound") |>   mutate(model = recode(model,     classical = "Classical: |β| ≤ C",     mape_svr  = "MAPE-SVR: |β| ≤ 100C/y"   )) |>   ggplot(aes(y, bound, colour = model, linetype = model)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(vpal[2], vpal[5]), name = NULL) +   scale_linetype_manual(values = c("dashed", "solid"), name = NULL) +   labs(x = "Scaled target yₖ  (Demand / 1e5)", y = "Box constraint bound",        title = "Box constraints: classical vs MAPE-SVR  (C = 5)",        subtitle = "MAPE-SVR tightens constraints for lower-demand days") +   theme(legend.position = "top")

text: LS-SVR variant (Model 3) replaces ε-insensitive loss squared loss, turning QP linear system. percentage-error variant modifies regularization diagonal: YΓ=diag(y12Γ,…,yN2Γ)Y_\Gamma = \text{diag}\!\left(\frac{y_1^2}{\Gamma}, \ldots, \frac{y_N^2}{\Gamma}\right) target-weighted diagonal causes system minimise root mean squared percentage error (RMSPE) rather standard squared residual. key advantage: solution requires base::solve() (N+1)×(N+1)(N+1)\times(N+1) system — QP solver needed.

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: Six features describe day: temperature (linear quadratic), day week, month, weekend flag, public holiday flag. use strict temporal split — 2012–2013 training, 2014 testing — avoid look-ahead bias. Figure 5: Train/test temporal split. grey band marks 2014 (test set). Colour encodes IsWeekend. Temporal leakage split calendar year, never shuffling. random split allow 2014 observations leak training, giving optimistically biased test metrics reflect operational forecast accuracy.
code:
feature_cols <- c("Temperature", "Temperature2", "DayOfWeek",                   "Month", "IsWeekend", "IsHoliday")  train <- elec_daily |> filter(year(Date) <= 2013)   # strict temporal split; no shuffling test  <- elec_daily |> filter(year(Date) == 2014)  X_raw_tr <- as.matrix(train[, feature_cols]) y_tr     <- train$Demand X_raw_te <- as.matrix(test[, feature_cols]) y_te     <- test$Demand  # Standardise using training statistics only (apply same shift/scale to test set) cm   <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) cs   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd) X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = cm, scale = cs) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = cm, scale = cs)  # Scale targets to ~[1.6, 3.5] for numerical conditioning; multiply back after prediction y_scale <- 1e5 y_tr_s  <- y_tr / y_scale y_te_s  <- y_te / y_scale  cat(sprintf("Train: %d days  (%s – %s)
",             nrow(train), min(train$Date), max(train$Date))) Train: 732 days  (2011-12-31 – 2013-12-31) cat(sprintf("Test:  %d days  (%s – %s)
",             nrow(test),  min(test$Date),  max(test$Date))) Test:  365 days  (2014-01-01 – 2014-12-31) cat(sprintf("Scaled target range (train): %.3f – %.3f
",             min(y_tr_s), max(y_tr_s))) Scaled target range (train): 0.825 – 3.162 cat(sprintf("Scaled target range (test):  %.3f – %.3f
",             min(y_te_s), max(y_te_s))) Scaled target range (test):  1.050 – 3.515 elec_daily |>   mutate(split = if_else(year(Date) <= 2013, "Train (2012–2013)", "Test (2014)"),          Weekend = factor(IsWeekend, labels = c("Weekday", "Weekend"))) |>   ggplot(aes(Date, Demand / 1e3, colour = Weekend)) +   annotate("rect", xmin = as.Date("2014-01-01"), xmax = as.Date("2014-12-31"),            ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = "grey90", alpha = 0.5) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.35, alpha = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(vpal[2], vpal[5]), name = NULL) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +   annotate("text", x = as.Date("2014-07-01"), y = 315,            label = "Test (2014)", colour = "grey40", size = 3.5) +   labs(x = NULL, y = "Demand (GWh/day)",        title = "Temporal train/test split",        subtitle = "Shaded region = test set") +   theme(legend.position = "top")

code: fit_base <- ksvm(                             # kernlab eps-SVR, uniform |beta_k| <= C for all k   X_tr, y_tr_s,   type    = "eps-svr",   kernel  = "rbfdot",   kpar    = list(sigma = 1),                  # sigma=1: exp(-3)≈0.05, useful for 6-dim std. data   C       = 10,   epsilon = 0.05,   scaled  = FALSE                             # already standardised externally ) fit_base Support Vector Machine object of class "ksvm"  SV type: eps-svr  (regression)  parameter : epsilon = 0.05  cost C = 10  Gaussian Radial Basis kernel function.  Hyperparameter : sigma =  1  Number of Support Vectors : 365  Objective Function Value : -57.8326 Training error : 0.00271 pred_base <- as.numeric(predict(fit_base, X_te)) * y_scale   # back to MWh  metrics_base <- tibble(   Model = "Baseline (ksvm)",   MAPE  = mape_fn(y_te,  pred_base),   RMSPE = rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_base),   R2    = r2_fn(y_te,   pred_base),   MSE   = mse_fn(y_te,  pred_base) ) tibble(actual = y_te / 1e3, pred = pred_base / 1e3,        date   = test$Date,        month  = factor(month(test$Date, label = TRUE))) |>   ggplot(aes(actual, pred, colour = month)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +   geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "grey30", linetype = "dashed") +   scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "D", name = "Month") +   scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +   labs(x = "Actual demand (GWh/day)", y = "Predicted demand (GWh/day)",        title = "Baseline: classical ε-SVR",        subtitle = sprintf("MAPE = %.2f%%   R² = %.4f",                           metrics_base$MAPE, metrics_base$R2)) +   coord_equal() +   theme(legend.position = "right")

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: Model 3 replaces uniform regularisation standard LS-SVR target-weighted diagonal term YΓ=diag(y12/Γ,…,yN2/Γ)Y_\Gamma = \text{diag}(y_1^2/\Gamma, \ldots, y_N^2/\Gamma), causing linear system directly minimise root mean squared percentage error. Mathematical detail: Theorem 3 linear system RMSPE LS-SVR primal minimises 12∥ω∥2+Γ2∑kek2/yk2\frac{1}{2}\|\omega\|^2 + \frac{\Gamma}{2}\sum_k e_k^2/y_k^2 subject yk=ω⊤ϕ(xk)+b+eky_k = \omega^\top\phi(x_k) + b + e_k. KKT stationarity collapses (N+1)×(N+1)(N+1)\times(N+1) system: [0𝟏⊤𝟏Ω+YΓ][bα]=[0𝐲] \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \mathbf{1}^\top \\ \mathbf{1} & \Omega + Y_\Gamma \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} b \\ \alpha \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \mathbf{y} \end{bmatrix} YΓ=diag(y12/Γ,…,yN2/Γ)Y_\Gamma = \text{diag}(y_1^2/\Gamma, \ldots, y_N^2/\Gamma) added diagonal kernel matrix Ω\Omega. Prediction: f(x)=∑kαkK(xk,x)+bf(x) = \sum_k \alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b. Figure 7: Model 3 (LS-SVR RMSPE) vs baseline: predicted actual demand 2014 test set.
code: K3 <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 2)    # closure accepts negated inputs for symmetric models  fit_m3 <- rmspe_lssvr(                 # solves (N+1)x(N+1) linear system via base::solve()   X_tr, y_tr_s,   kernel = K3,   gamma  = 500                         # larger Gamma → YGamma shrinks → tighter fit )  print(fit_m3)                          # reports kernel, Gamma, and training size LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe]    Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 2)   Gamma:         500   Training obs.: 732 cf_m3 <- coef(fit_m3) # alpha: N dual variables weighted by alpha_k in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k,x) + b # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs (LS-SVR has no sparsity) cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf_m3$b, min(cf_m3$alpha), max(cf_m3$alpha))) b = 2.1212  |  alpha range: [-42.5000, 22.5903] pred_m3 <- predict(fit_m3, X_te) * y_scale  metrics_m3 <- tibble(   Model = "Model 3 — LS-SVR RMSPE",   MAPE  = mape_fn(y_te,  pred_m3),   RMSPE = rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m3),   R2    = r2_fn(y_te,   pred_m3),   MSE   = mse_fn(y_te,  pred_m3) ) bind_rows(   tibble(actual = y_te / 1e3, pred = pred_base / 1e3, model = "Baseline (ksvm)"),   tibble(actual = y_te / 1e3, pred = pred_m3   / 1e3, model = "Model 3 (RMSPE)") ) |>   ggplot(aes(actual, pred, colour = model)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.35, size = 1.2) +   geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "grey30", linetype = "dashed") +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(vpal[2], vpal[5]), name = NULL) +   scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +   labs(x = "Actual (GWh/day)", y = "Predicted (GWh/day)",        title = "Baseline vs Model 3",        subtitle = sprintf("Model 3 — MAPE: %.2f%%   R²: %.4f",                           metrics_m3$MAPE, metrics_m3$R2)) +   coord_equal() +   theme(legend.position = "top")

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: Model 1 solves full quadratic program sample-dependent box constraints |βk|≤100C/yk|\beta_k| \leq 100C/y_k. ε-insensitivity tube also scaled proportionally, low-demand winter day requires ε% accuracy just much peak summer day. Computational note N=732N = 732 training points, QP 2N=14642N = 1464 variables 1464×14641464\times 1464 P matrix — feasible seconds via osqp ADMM solver. Using hourly data (N≈17,000N \approx 17{,}000) create ∼34,000×34,000\sim\!34{,}000\times 34{,}000 dense matrix requiring several GB RAM potentially hours compute. Daily aggregation practical choice solver. Mathematical detail: Theorem 1 QP formulation MAPE ε-SVR dual (u=[α;α*]∈ℝ2Nu = [\alpha;\alpha^*]\\mathbb{R}^{2N}) : minu12u⊤Pu+q⊤u \min_{u} \quad \tfrac{1}{2} u^\top P u + q^\top u P=[Ω−Ω−ΩΩ],q=[y(ε/100−1)y(1+ε/100)] P = \begin{bmatrix} \Omega & -\Omega \\ -\Omega & \Omega \end{bmatrix}, \quad q = \begin{bmatrix} y(\varepsilon/100 - 1) \\ y(1 + \varepsilon/100) \end{bmatrix} subject [𝟏⊤,−𝟏⊤]u=0[\mathbf{1}^\top, -\mathbf{1}^\top]\,u = 0 0≤αk,αk*≤100C/yk0 \leq \alpha_k,\,\alpha_k^* \leq 100C/y_k. solving: βk=αk−αk*\beta_k = \alpha_k - \alpha_k^*, f(x)=∑kβkK(xk,x)+bf(x) = \sum_k \beta_k K(x_k, x) + b. Figure 8: Distribution support vector targets Model 1. Support vectors cluster per-sample box constraint still allows |β| > 0 — .e. MAPE loss hardest minimise.
code: K1 <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)   # sigma=1 chosen by grid search  fit_m1 <- mape_svr(                   # 2Nx2N QP via osqp (ADMM), P as sparse dgCMatrix   X_tr, y_tr_s,   kernel = K1,   C      = 5,                         # per-sample bound: |beta_k| <= 100*5/y_k   eps    = 2                          # tube: ±2% of each scaled target → zero loss )  print(fit_m1)                         # reports support vector count and hyperparameters Epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss  [psvr_mape]    Kernel:          RBF (sigma = 1)   C:               5   eps:             2   Training obs.:   732   Support vectors: 375 (51.2%) cf_m1 <- coef(fit_m1) # alpha: beta_k = alpha_k - alpha_k* per support vector (non-zero outside ε-tube, sparse) # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  training rows for support vectors only cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf_m1$b, min(cf_m1$alpha), max(cf_m1$alpha))) b = 2.1185  |  alpha range: [-291.1579, 259.9121] pred_m1 <- predict(fit_m1, X_te) * y_scale  metrics_m1 <- tibble(   Model = "Model 1 — ε-SVR MAPE",   MAPE  = mape_fn(y_te,  pred_m1),   RMSPE = rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m1),   R2    = r2_fn(y_te,   pred_m1),   MSE   = mse_fn(y_te,  pred_m1) ) tibble(y_sv = fit_m1$y_sv * y_scale / 1e3) |>   ggplot(aes(y_sv)) +   geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = vpal[4], colour = "white", alpha = 0.85) +   scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +   labs(x = "Support vector demand (GWh/day)", y = "Count",        title = "Model 1: support vector demand distribution",        subtitle = sprintf("%d support vectors out of %d training days (%.1f%%)",                           length(fit_m1$beta), fit_m1$n_train,                           100 * length(fit_m1$beta) / fit_m1$n_train))

text: symmetric models (Models 2 4) add kernel symmetry constraint respective primal problems: ω⊤ϕ(xk)=⋅ω⊤ϕ(−xk),∈{−1,1} \omega^\top \phi(x_k) = \cdot \omega^\top \phi(-x_k), \quad \\{-1, 1\} =1a=1 (even symmetry), model forced give prediction standardized feature vector xx negation −x-x. key theoretical result constraint can absorbed modified symmetric kernel: Ks(xi,xj)=K(xi,xj)+⋅K(xi,−xj) K_s(x_i, x_j) = K(x_i, x_j) + \cdot K(x_i, -x_j) Symmetric kernels Assumption 3 symmetry constraint requires kernel satisfy K(−xi,xj)=K(xi,−xj)K(-x_i, x_j) = K(x_i, -x_j) (Assumption 3 paper). RBF kernel satisfies ∥−xi−xj∥2=∥xi+xj∥2=∥xi−(−xj)∥2\|-x_i - x_j\|^2 = \|x_i + x_j\|^2 = \|x_i - (-x_j)\|^2. means negating input vector break kernel computation, even training data non-negative original space. standardized feature space, negative coordinates perfectly plausible (-average temperature, early months, weekdays, etc.). context daily electricity demand, symmetry constraint acts additional regularizer restricts function class. aligns data’s structure can improve generalization; , may slightly hurt performance. grid search results Section 9 provide empirical evidence dataset. Mathematical detail: Theorem 4 linear system structure Model 3 Ωs\Omega_s replacing Ω\Omega: [0𝟏⊤𝟏Ωs+YΓ][bα]=[0𝐲] \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \mathbf{1}^\top \\ \mathbf{1} & \Omega_s + Y_\Gamma \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} b \\ \alpha \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \mathbf{y} \end{bmatrix} Prediction: f(x)=∑kαk12[K(xk,x)+aK(xk,−x)]+bf(x) = \sum_k \alpha_k \tfrac{1}{2}[K(x_k,x) + \,K(x_k,-x)] + b. factor 12\tfrac{1}{2} arises symmetric representer theorem (Appendix .4 paper). full derivations symmetric kernel extensions see Theorems 2 4 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026).
code: K4 <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 2)   # same kernel as Model 3  fit_m4 <- rmspe_sym_lssvr(            # Omega replaced by Omega_s = 0.5*(Omega + a*Omega*)   X_tr, y_tr_s,   kernel = K4,   gamma  = 500,   a      = 1                          # even symmetry: f(x) = f(-x) ) print(fit_m4) Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe_sym]    Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 2)   Gamma:         500   Symmetry:      even  (a = 1)   Training obs.: 732 cf_m4 <- coef(fit_m4) # alpha: N dual variables; f(x) = sum_k alpha_k * Ks(x_k,x)/2 + b # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs (symmetric LS-SVR has no sparsity) cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf_m4$b, min(cf_m4$alpha), max(cf_m4$alpha))) b = 2.2273  |  alpha range: [-41.3483, 24.0013] pred_m4   <- predict(fit_m4, X_te) * y_scale metrics_m4 <- tibble(   Model = "Model 4 — Sym LS-SVR RMSPE",   MAPE  = mape_fn(y_te,  pred_m4),   RMSPE = rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m4),   R2    = r2_fn(y_te,   pred_m4),   MSE   = mse_fn(y_te,  pred_m4) ) K2 <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)   # same kernel as Model 1  fit_m2 <- mape_sym_svr(               # Ks = Omega + a*Omega*, P = (1/4)*Ks in QP (Appendix A.2)   X_tr, y_tr_s,   kernel = K2,   C      = 5,   eps    = 2,   a      = 1 ) print(fit_m2) Symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss  [psvr_mape_sym]    Kernel:          RBF (sigma = 1)   C:               5   eps:             2   Symmetry:        even  (a = 1)   Training obs.:   732   Support vectors: 369 (50.4%) cf_m2 <- coef(fit_m2) # alpha: beta_k = alpha_k - alpha_k* per support vector (sparse) # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  training rows for support vectors only cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf_m2$b, min(cf_m2$alpha), max(cf_m2$alpha))) b = 2.1620  |  alpha range: [-291.1579, 259.9121] pred_m2   <- predict(fit_m2, X_te) * y_scale metrics_m2 <- tibble(   Model = "Model 2 — Sym ε-SVR MAPE",   MAPE  = mape_fn(y_te,  pred_m2),   RMSPE = rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m2),   R2    = r2_fn(y_te,   pred_m2),   MSE   = mse_fn(y_te,  pred_m2) )

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: tutorial demonstrated psvr package real-world daily electricity demand forecasting task using three years data Victoria, Australia. four percentage-error SVR models — LS-SVR RMSPE (Model 3), ε-SVR MAPE (Model 1), symmetric kernel extensions (Models 2 4) — fitted 2012–2013 data evaluated full 2014 hold-year. percentage-error models achieve MAPE values 3.6–4.1%, outperforming classical ε-SVR baseline (MAPE 4.1%) RMSPE-minimising models. Model 3 (LS-SVR RMSPE) achieves best MAPE 3.58%, confirming theoretical motivation: relative accuracy matters — electricity markets forecast errors penalised proportionally — aligning loss function forecasting objective measurably improves performance. symmetric kernel extensions (Models 2 4) provide principled way inject structural constraint model. dataset, Model 2 slightly improves upon Model 1 MAPE, Model 4 marginally Model 3 — illustrating value symmetry constraint depends whether aligns data’s geometry standardized feature space. practice, symmetry type (∈{−1,1}\\{-1,1\}) hyperparameters selected via time-series cross-validation (e.g., rolling-window scheme) respect temporal structure data. Full mathematical derivations, KKT conditions, proofs unified framework available Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026, review). psvr package provides production-ready R implementations four models together tidymodels integration via parsnip seamless use modelling pipelines.

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), misaligned scale-free accuracy criteria standard forecasting. error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 large 2. psvr implements four SVR variants derived percentage-error loss functions (Benavides-Herrera et al., 2026): models require strictly positive targets (y > 0), condition percentage residuals well-defined.

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: # CRAN (once released) install.packages("psvr")  # Development version from GitHub remotes::install_github("pbenavidesh/psvr")

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: reproduce experiments reported Tables 1–2 paper using Boston Housing dataset (Harrison & Rubinfeld, 1978). 506 observations strictly positive median home values (MEDV, thousands USD), making MAPE well-defined throughout.
code: library(psvr) library(ggplot2)  # Boston Housing is available in the MASS package data("Boston", package = "MASS")  # Target: median home value (all > 0) y_all <- Boston$medv X_raw <- as.matrix(Boston[, setdiff(names(Boston), "medv")])  stopifnot(all(y_all > 0)) cat("N =", nrow(X_raw), "  p =", ncol(X_raw),     "  y range: [", round(min(y_all), 1), ",", round(max(y_all), 1), "]\n") #> N = 506   p = 13   y range: [ 5 , 50 ]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Features standardised using training-set statistics RBF kernel operates comparable scale across 13 predictors.
code: set.seed(42) n      <- nrow(X_raw) tr_idx <- sample(n, floor(0.7 * n))  X_raw_tr <- X_raw[tr_idx, ];  y_tr <- y_all[tr_idx] X_raw_te <- X_raw[-tr_idx, ]; y_te <- y_all[-tr_idx]  # Standardise: centre and scale by training mean/sd col_mean <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) col_sd   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd) X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd)

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: mape  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100 rmspe <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean(((y - yhat) / y)^2)) * 100 r2    <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2)

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: lm_df_tr <- as.data.frame(X_tr) lm_df_te <- as.data.frame(X_te) lm_fit   <- lm(y_tr ~ ., data = lm_df_tr) lm_pred  <- predict(lm_fit, newdata = lm_df_te)  cat(sprintf("Linear regression — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, lm_pred), rmspe(y_te, lm_pred), r2(y_te, lm_pred))) #> Linear regression — MAPE: 16.29%  RMSPE: 22.48%  R²: 0.7730

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: LS-SVR formulation replaces QP linear system using quadratic penalty percentage residuals. dual reduces : [0𝟏⊤𝟏Ω+YΓ][b𝛂]=[0𝐲] \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \mathbf{1}^\top \\ \mathbf{1} & \Omega + Y_\Gamma \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} b \\ \boldsymbol{\alpha} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \mathbf{y} \end{bmatrix} YΓ=diag(y12/Γ,…,yN2/Γ)Y_\Gamma = \operatorname{diag}(y_1^2/\Gamma, \ldots, y_N^2/\Gamma).
code: # make_kernel() returns a closure K(xi, xj) = exp(-||xi - xj||^2 / (2 sigma^2)) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # gamma = 5000: regularisation; larger gamma -> smaller Y_Gamma diagonal -> tighter fit fit_ls <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 5000) pred_ls <- predict(fit_ls, X_te)  cat(sprintf("LS-SVR RMSPE  — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, pred_ls), rmspe(y_te, pred_ls), r2(y_te, pred_ls))) #> LS-SVR RMSPE  — MAPE: 15.46%  RMSPE: 27.21%  R²: 0.7277 print(fit_ls) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe] #>  #>   Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 1) #>   Gamma:         5000 #>   Training obs.: 354 cf_ls <- coef(fit_ls) # alpha: N dual variables; weight each training point's kernel contribution #        in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b (all N points, no sparsity) # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs stored for prediction cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf_ls$b, min(cf_ls$alpha), max(cf_ls$alpha))) #> b = 22.5268  |  alpha range: [-59.7536, 43.7327]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: ε-SVR formulation optimises QP sample-dependent box constraints |βk|≤100C/yk|\beta_k| \le 100C/y_k: tighter bounds small targets, concentrating model capacity low-magnitude observations.
code:
# C = 10: per-sample box bound |beta_k| <= 100*C/y_k; eps = 1: tube width (% of y_k) fit_ep <- mape_svr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 1) pred_ep <- predict(fit_ep, X_te)  cat(sprintf("ε-SVR MAPE    — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f
",             mape(y_te, pred_ep), rmspe(y_te, pred_ep), r2(y_te, pred_ep))) #> ε-SVR MAPE    — MAPE: 15.77%  RMSPE: 27.54%  R²: 0.7617 cat(sprintf("Support vectors: %d / %d
", length(fit_ep$beta), nrow(X_tr))) #> Support vectors: 328 / 354 print(fit_ep) #>  #> Epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss  [psvr_mape] #>  #>   Kernel:          RBF (sigma = 1) #>   C:               10 #>   eps:             1 #>   Training obs.:   354 #>   Support vectors: 328 (92.7%) cf_ep <- coef(fit_ep) # alpha: beta_k = alpha_k - alpha_k* for each support vector; non-zero only for #        training points outside the percentage-error ε-tube (sparse) # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  training rows corresponding to support vectors only cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             cf_ep$b, min(cf_ep$alpha), max(cf_ep$alpha))) #> b = 23.1097  |  alpha range: [-111.5553, 82.6446]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Test-set performance Boston Housing (70/30 split, RBF kernel, single run). psvr models improve linear baseline respective percentage-error objectives. LS-SVR formulation (Model 3) minimises RMSPE directly achieves lowest squared-percentage error; ε-SVR formulation (Model 1) targets MAPE sample-dependent box constraints dual variables. results consistent Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026), SVR-MAPE reduces test-set MAPE 15.23% (classical ε-SVR) around 10% relative linear baseline 17.85%.
code: results <- data.frame(   Model = c("Linear regression", "LS-SVR RMSPE (Model 3)",             "\u03b5-SVR MAPE (Model 1)"),   MAPE  = c(mape(y_te,  lm_pred),             mape(y_te,  pred_ls),             mape(y_te,  pred_ep)),   RMSPE = c(rmspe(y_te, lm_pred),             rmspe(y_te, pred_ls),             rmspe(y_te, pred_ep)),   R2    = c(r2(y_te,    lm_pred),             r2(y_te,    pred_ls),             r2(y_te,    pred_ep)) ) results[, 2:4] <- round(results[, 2:4], 2) knitr::kable(results, col.names = c("Model", "MAPE (%)", "RMSPE (%)", "R²"),              align = "lrrr",              caption = paste("Test-set performance on Boston Housing",                              "(70/30 split, RBF kernel, single run)."))

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: vignette verifies R implementations psvr reproduce numerical results reported Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026), originally computed Python using cvxopt (Models 1 & 2) numpy.linalg.lstsq (Models 3 & 4). verification strategy : Use dataset, split seed, hyperparameters Python experiments. Report four metrics: MAPE, RMSPE, R², MSE test set. Compare R outputs paper reference values. Small numerical differences expected acceptable: Python implementation uses cvxopt QP (Models 1 & 2) pseudoinverse via numpy.linalg.lstsq linear system (Models 3 & 4), whereas psvr uses osqp base::solve(), respectively. approaches solve mathematical problem use different numerical algorithms different stopping tolerances, can produce discrepancies order 0.01–0.1 percentage points reported metrics.

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Hyperparameters Table 1 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026): Python RBF kernel uses gamma_py convention K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp(−γpy∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2)K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma_{\text{py}} \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2), equivalent psvr RBF convention K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp(−∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2/2σ2)K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2 / 2\sigma^2) σ=1/(2γpy)\sigma = \sqrt{1 / (2\,\gamma_{\text{py}})}.

sigma_m3 <- sqrt(1 / (2 * 0.050967)) cat("Model 3 — sigma:", round(sigma_m3, 6), "
") #> Model 3 — sigma: 3.132135  K3  <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = sigma_m3) fit_m3 <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K3, gamma = 16962.540770) pred_m3 <- predict(fit_m3, X_te)  m3_mape  <- mape_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_rmspe <- rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_r2    <- r2_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_mse   <- mse_fn(y_te, pred_m3)  cat(sprintf(   "Model 3 (R) — MAPE: %.4f%%  RMSPE: %.4f%%  R²: %.6f  MSE: %.6f
",   m3_mape, m3_rmspe, m3_r2, m3_mse )) #> Model 3 (R) — MAPE: 11.0496%  RMSPE: 18.7205%  R²: 0.819582  MSE: 14.127739 print(fit_m3) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe] #>  #>   Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 3.13213) #>   Gamma:         16962.5 #>   Training obs.: 354 cf_m3 <- coef(fit_m3) # alpha: N dual variables; weight each training point in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs (LS-SVR — no sparsity, every point contributes) cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             cf_m3$b, min(cf_m3$alpha), max(cf_m3$alpha))) #> b = 25.1360  |  alpha range: [-774.6276, 307.9745]

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: observed gaps (e.g. R² 0.82 vs 0.90 Model 3) evidence implementation bug. Numerical investigation confirms three things: Non-equivalent splits dominant cause. set.seed(4) seeds R’s Mersenne Twister; random_state=4 seeds numpy’s Mersenne Twister. independent generators happen share API convention seed integer produce entirely different random sequences. 152 test observations selected different, metrics computed different data. linear solver factor. Replacing base::solve() MASS::ginv() (pseudoinverse via SVD, matching Python’s numpy.linalg.lstsq) changes predictions 3 × 10⁻¹² dataset. augmented system condition number κ ≈ 2 100, LU decomposition SVD agree machine precision. QP solver minor factor. osqp (ADMM) cvxopt (interior-point) solve QP different tolerances, producing slightly different dual variables. accounts 0.1–0.2 percentage points MAPE, multi-point gaps seen . correct way verify mathematical equivalence KKT conditions: unit tests tests/testthat/ check fitted models satisfy KKT stationarity, complementary slackness, feasibility conditions within solver tolerance, independently particular split.

code: best_params <- select_best(tune_res, metric = "mape") best_params #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>    cost rbf_sigma .config          #>   <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>            #> 1   100       0.5 pre0_mod13_post0

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr

code: final_wf  <- finalize_workflow(wf, best_params) final_fit <- last_fit(final_wf, split, metrics = metric_set(mape))  collect_metrics(final_fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   .metric .estimator .estimate .config         #>   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl> <chr>           #> 1 mape    standard        5.24 pre0_mod0_post0 preds <- collect_predictions(final_fit) head(preds[, c(".row", "y", ".pred")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>    .row     y .pred #>   <int> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1     3  5.99  6.00 #> 2     4  6.20  6.25 #> 3     5  4.69  4.90 #> 4     6  1.96  2.14 #> 5     8  6.70  6.87 #> 6     9  3.93  3.96 new_obs <- data.frame(x1 = c(0, 1, -2), x2 = c(0, 1, 2)) predict(extract_workflow(final_fit), new_data = new_obs) #> # A tibble: 3 × 1 #>   .pred #>   <dbl> #> 1  2.06 #> 2  3.51 #> 3  8.15

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: tidymodels layer wraps psvr_rmspe object (returned rmspe_lssvr()). Extract use print() coef() directly.
code: # extract_fit_engine() unwraps the parsnip/workflow layer to the raw psvr object psvr_fit <- extract_fit_engine(extract_workflow(final_fit)) print(psvr_fit) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe] #>  #>   Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 0.5) #>   Gamma:         100 #>   Training obs.: 150 cf <- coef(psvr_fit) # alpha: N dual variables; weight each training point in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs (LS-SVR has no sparsity — every training point contributes) cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf$b, min(cf$alpha), max(cf$alpha))) #> b = 7.6271  |  alpha range: [-3.5482, 2.1956]

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: psvr implements four support vector regression models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), scale-dependent: error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 critical 2. psvr addresses optimising MAPE RMSPE directly, making well-suited forecasting tasks targets strictly positive relative accuracy matters. models require strictly positive targets (y > 0).

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
code: # Development version from GitHub remotes::install_github("pbenavidesh/psvr")

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: full worked example Boston Housing dataset — including 70/30 train–test split, feature standardisation, comparison linear baseline — see vignette("getting-started", package = "psvr").
code:
library(psvr)  # Synthetic dataset: even function (f(-x) = f(x)), targets strictly positive set.seed(42) n  <- 100 X  <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) y  <- 2 + X[, 1]^2 + 0.5 * X[, 2]^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.1)  tr <- 1:70;  te <- 71:100 X_tr <- X[tr, ];  y_tr <- y[tr] X_te <- X[te, ];  y_te <- y[te]  # Standardise features using training-set statistics (important for RBF) col_mean <- colMeans(X_tr);  col_sd <- apply(X_tr, 2, sd) X_tr_s   <- scale(X_tr, col_mean, col_sd) X_te_s   <- scale(X_te, col_mean, col_sd)  K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # Model 1 — ε-SVR with MAPE fit1  <- mape_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5) pred1 <- predict(fit1, X_te_s)  # Model 2 — Symmetric ε-SVR with MAPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit2  <- mape_sym_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5, a = 1) pred2 <- predict(fit2, X_te_s)  # Model 3 — LS-SVR with RMSPE fit3  <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100) pred3 <- predict(fit3, X_te_s)  # Model 4 — Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit4  <- rmspe_sym_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100, a = 1) pred4 <- predict(fit4, X_te_s)  mape <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100  cat(sprintf("Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred1))) cat(sprintf("Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred2))) cat(sprintf("Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred3))) cat(sprintf("Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred4))) #> Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = 3.59% #> Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = 6.04% #> Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = 3.76% #> Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = 5.79%

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: function K(xi, xj) xi xj numeric vectors length, returning scalar kernel evaluation.

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: three supported kernels : RBF: K(xi, xj) = exp(-‖xi - xj‖² / (2 * sigma²)) Linear: K(xi, xj) = xi · xj Polynomial: K(xi, xj) = (xi · xj + coef0)^degree RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy Assumption 3 paper (kernel symmetry), making compatible symmetric models. linear kernel odd-degree polynomial kernels satisfy Assumption 3 used mape_sym_svr() rmspe_sym_lssvr().

C Regularization parameter C > 0. eps Insensitivity tube half-width ε ≥ 0 (percentage units). Symmetry parameter: 1 even symmetry f(x) = f(-x), -1 odd symmetry f(x) = -f(-x). tol Threshold |βk| treated zero (default 1e-5).

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: dual variables u = [α; α*] ∈ R^{2N} : P = ½ · [Ks, -Ks; -Ks, Ks] osqp's ½ uᵀPu evaluates ¼ βᵀKsβ, matching −¼ coefficient Theorem 2. q = [y(ε/100 − 1); y(1 + ε/100)] (identical Model 1) Equality: [1ᵀ, −1ᵀ] u = 0 Box: 0 ≤ αk ≤ 100C/yk, 0 ≤ αk* ≤ 100C/yk (identical Model 1) Note: Ks = Ω + ·Ω* carries ½ factor . ½ lives P osqp's internal ½ produces required ¼ overall. title: psvr 0.0.0.9001
text: Expanded parsnip integration 4 12 model specs following tidymodels pattern used svm_rbf(), svm_poly(), svm_linear(): psvr_mape_rbf(), psvr_mape_poly(), psvr_mape_linear() — Model 1 specs. psvr_mape_sym_rbf(), psvr_mape_sym_poly(), psvr_mape_sym_linear() — Model 2 specs. psvr_rmspe_rbf(), psvr_rmspe_poly(), psvr_rmspe_linear() — Model 3 specs. psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(), psvr_rmspe_sym_poly(), psvr_rmspe_sym_linear() — Model 4 specs. Kernel parameters now tunable parsnip args mapped existing dials params: rbf_sigma → dials::rbf_sigma(), degree → dials::degree(), scale_factor → dials::scale_factor(). kernel closure built inside fit wrapper longer appears parsnip layer. old single-spec API (psvr_mape(), psvr_mape_sym(), psvr_rmspe(), psvr_rmspe_sym()) removed. Migrate replacing, example, psvr_rmspe(cost = tune()) |> set_engine("psvr", kernel = K) psvr_rmspe_rbf(cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = 1) |> set_engine("psvr").

title: psvr 0.0.0.9001
text: vignettes pkgdown articles updated new spec-based API. 12 new smoke tests (fit + predict) specs tests/testthat/test-parsnip.R. pkgdown articles reorganised three named groups: Get Started, Case Studies, Technical Notes.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: Initial development release.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: mape_svr() — epsilon-SVR MAPE loss (Model 1). Solves dual QP via osqp per-sample box constraints |βk| ≤ 100C/yₖ. mape_sym_svr() — symmetric epsilon-SVR MAPE loss (Model 2). Enforces f(x) = ·f(-x) replacing kernel symmetric kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj). rmspe_lssvr() — LS-SVR RMSPE loss (Model 3). Solves (N+1)×(N+1) linear system directly via base::solve(). rmspe_sym_lssvr() — symmetric LS-SVR RMSPE loss (Model 4). linear system Model 3 symmetrized kernel matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + ·Ω*). four models derived unified percentage-error loss framework; see Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) mathematical proofs.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: make_kernel() — factory returning kernel closure type ∈ {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy symmetry assumption (Assumption 3) required Models 2 4.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: Four parsnip model specifications use within tidymodels workflows: psvr_mape() — spec Model 1; hyperparameters cost svm_margin. psvr_mape_sym() — spec Model 2; hyperparameters cost svm_margin. psvr_rmspe() — spec Model 3; hyperparameter cost (maps Γ). psvr_rmspe_sym() — spec Model 4; hyperparameter cost (maps Γ). kernel (symmetric models) symmetry parameter engine arguments supplied via set_engine("psvr", kernel = ..., = ...). Hyperparameters map dials::cost() dials::svm_margin() compatibility tune_grid().

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: 84 unit tests covering four models: kernel correctness, QP/linear-system solutions, support vector selection, bias recovery, predict consistency, input validation.
